metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add concrete proving commands (e.g. `terraform show -json tf.plan | jq -e` queries) for each remaining AC1 sub-claim: (a) integration URI starts with 'https://', (b) aws_apigatewayv2_integration has the X-Opcert-Guard static header parameter, (c) /rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key has lifecycle ignore_changes on value, (d) /rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard exists under the existing path, (e) hashicorp/random appears in .terraform_version or versions.tf required_providers.
findings.finding: AC1 provides a concrete jq proving command only for the IAM invoke-policy sub-claim; the remaining AC1 assertions — (a) https:// integration URI, (b) X-Opcert-Guard static request-header mapping on the integration, (c) /rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key SecureString with lifecycle ignore_changes, (d) /rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard SecureString under the existing path grant, (e) hashicorp/random in required_providers — are covered only by the vague phrase 'A test asserts these declarations exist' with no stated command or query.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1 (second and third sub-bullets after the jq block)
findings.suggested_fix:
Extend the AC1 test block with additional `terraform show -json tf.plan | jq -e` assertions, e.g.:
• URI: `... | select(.type=="aws_apigatewayv2_integration") | .values.integration_uri | startswith("https://")`
• Guard header: `... | select(.type=="aws_apigatewayv2_integration") | .values.request_parameters["overwrite:header.X-Opcert-Guard"]`
• Key lifecycle: `... | select(.type=="aws_ssm_parameter" and .values.name=="/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key") | .values.lifecycle.ignore_changes | contains(["value"])`
• Random provider: `grep -E '"random"' infra/terraform/.terraform.lock.hcl` (post-init) or check versions.tf directly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name and enumerate the script/runbook step that installs `infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template` → `/etc/nginx/conf.d/rebar.conf` (e.g. create `infra/scripts/install-nginx-conf.sh` or extend `install-certbot-timer.sh`), and declare it as an edit target so the deny-all `/etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf` install is wired into that same step.
findings.finding: The plan's boot-ordering section states the nginx-config install step 'ALSO installs a DENY-ALL default /etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf' but the step that installs rebar.conf from the template to /etc/nginx/conf.d/rebar.conf is not owned by any existing script in the repo (install-certbot-timer.sh references it only in a comment; compose-up.sh does not install it). The plan piggybacks on this 'nginx-config install step' without naming the script/runbook step that owns it or declaring it as an edit target, leaving the deny-all guard install unrooted.
findings.location: What section / Compose wiring / boot ordering hazard (a)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a self-failing structured assertion (e.g., a second `terraform show -json tf.plan | jq -e` command) to AC1 that validates the `aws_iam_role.rebar_opcert_admin` resource's `assume_role_policy` contains ONLY principals from `var.opcert_admin_principal_arns` and no wildcard principal.
findings.finding: The `rebar-opcert-admin` IAM role's trust policy (restricting which principals may assume the role via `opcert_admin_principal_arns`) is declared as a security property in AC1 but has no structured, self-failing assertion — the only machine-verifiable jq command in AC1 validates the *permission policy* (that exactly one `execute-api:Invoke` grant exists on the named resource), not the trust policy. An IaC defect that set the trust policy to `"*"` or `"arn:aws:iam::*:root"` would pass the AC1 test command while granting any AWS principal the ability to assume the admin role and invoke the opcert endpoint.
findings.location: AC1 — structured jq assertion; What section — 'trust policy limited to the principal ARNs supplied via a new variable opcert_admin_principal_arns'
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the AC1 jq command (or add a second command) such as: `terraform show -json tf.plan | jq -e '[.planned_values.root_module.resources[] | select(.type == "aws_iam_role" and .name == "rebar_opcert_admin")] | length == 1 and (.[0].values.assume_role_policy | fromjson | .Statement[] | .Principal | to_entries | all(.value != "*"))'`

findings.finding: The live access-control checks in AC5 test only (a) a correctly-signed request from the authorized role and (b) a completely unsigned/unauthenticated request. They do NOT test a SigV4-signed request from a valid-but-wrong AWS identity (i.e., an identity that IS authenticated but is NOT the `rebar-opcert-admin` role). This failure path is the critical one for verifying that the `execute-api:Invoke` grant is correctly scoped to exactly the IaC-created role — a misconfigured trust policy or an overly-broad `execute-api:Invoke` could make the wrong-role request succeed while the plan-level jq check still passes (it checks plan intent, not deployed policy enforcement).
findings.location: AC5 (live checks) and AC1 (Terraform declarations)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC5 with: 'a SigV4-signed request from a valid AWS identity that is NOT the rebar-opcert-admin role is rejected with 403 at API Gateway — evidence: captured response body/status alongside the invoking identity (aws sts get-caller-identity output).' This can be done by signing with the operator's base IAM user/role (before sts:AssumeRole to rebar-opcert-admin), which is already present in the authenticated AWS CLI session.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `prevent_destroy = true` inside `lifecycle` on the `aws_ssm_parameter` for `/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key` (the sole-copy, irreplaceable key), matching the existing `prevent_destroy` pattern on `aws_ebs_volume.data` and `aws_eip.gerrit`.
findings.finding: The SSM SecureString `/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key` is explicitly described as the sole copy of the key ('the key exists nowhere else') yet the plan does not declare `prevent_destroy` on it. A `terraform destroy -target` on that parameter — or a state rm + apply — silently and irreversibly destroys the only copy, with no Terraform safeguard.
findings.location: What section — SSM Parameter Store SecureString; AC1: 'the two SecureString parameters (the key parameter guarded by lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] })'
findings.suggested_fix:
In the new `aws_ssm_parameter` resource for `/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key`, use:
```hcl
lifecycle {
  ignore_changes  = [value]
  prevent_destroy = true
}
```
This matches the existing precedent on `aws_ebs_volume.data` for irreplaceable stateful resources.

findings.finding: The SSM SecureString `/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key` — explicitly described as the only copy of the Ed25519 private key — has `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` to prevent Terraform from overwriting the operator-seeded key, but the plan specifies NO `prevent_destroy` on this parameter. A `terraform destroy`, a state-level `terraform state rm` followed by re-apply, or accidental module deletion would destroy the SSM parameter and permanently lose the key with no recovery path.
findings.location: What section — SSM Parameter Store SecureString `/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key`; AC1 — 'the key parameter guarded by lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an assertion to AC #3 (or a new sub-bullet) that the nginx config install step ships a DENY-ALL default `/etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf` (e.g. `map $http_x_opcert_guard $opcert_guard_ok { default 0; }`) so the fail-closed boot property is explicitly tested.
findings.finding: The boot-safety property — that the nginx config install step ships a DENY-ALL default `/etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf` (so nginx starts fail-closed on `/opcert/` before `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` runs) — is described prominently in What but is not explicitly asserted by any AC. AC #3 asserts that the nginx route requires `X-Opcert-Guard` and that the materialize script renders the guard value, but does not assert the existence or content of the deny-all default file that provides the fail-closed boot guarantee. An implementer could omit this file and satisfy all ACs.
findings.location: AC #3 / What section (boot ordering hazard closure)
findings.suggested_fix: In AC #3, append: 'and that the nginx config installation (not the materialize script) ships a deny-all default `/etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf` containing only `map $http_x_opcert_guard $opcert_guard_ok { default 0; }` so that `/opcert/` returns 403 if the materialize script has not yet run.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an assertion in AC3 (or a dedicated test) that verifies: (a) the nginx config install step ships a syntactically-valid deny-all default `/etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf` (e.g. assert the file content matches `map $http_x_opcert_guard $opcert_guard_ok { default 0; }`) and (b) `rebar.conf.template` contains both the `include /etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf;` directive and the `/opcert/` location block requiring the guard, so the fail-closed boot invariant is verified without a live deploy.
findings.finding: The fail-closed boot invariant — that a DENY-ALL `/etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf` is installed before nginx starts so a fresh boot serves 403 on /opcert/ rather than nginx failing on a missing include — is explicitly specified in the What section as a safety property, but no Acceptance Criterion asserts that this default file is part of the nginx-config installation artifacts or that the template install step ships it.
findings.location: AC3 (compose/nginx test assertions); What section — 'Two ordering hazards are closed explicitly: (a) BOOT'
findings.suggested_fix: In the compose/nginx test (AC3), add two assertions: (1) parse `infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template` and assert it contains `include /etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf;` and a `location /opcert/` block with the guard check; (2) assert that the nginx-config install script (or a tracked file at `infra/nginx/opcert-guard.conf.default` or similar) ships the deny-all map content `map $http_x_opcert_guard $opcert_guard_ok { default 0; }` — confirming the fail-closed default is part of the artifact set, not just documented intent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the precedent claim: remove `materialize-deploy-key.sh` from the list of scripts called by `compose-up.sh` (it is called from `setup-replication.sh`); update the plan to cite only `materialize-g2p-config.sh` as the compose-up.sh wiring precedent.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `materialize-deploy-key.sh` is called from `compose-up.sh` as a precedent for wiring `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` into the boot orchestrator ('WIRED INTO the boot orchestrator infra/scripts/compose-up.sh alongside the existing materialize/.env-regeneration calls, running BEFORE docker compose up' — citing 'infra/gerrit/materialize-deploy-key.sh / materialize-g2p-config.sh'). Reading compose-up.sh confirms only `materialize-g2p-config.sh` is called there (line 177); `materialize-deploy-key.sh` is invoked from `infra/gerrit/setup-replication.sh`, not compose-up.sh. The cited two-script precedent is half-wrong, which means the plan's claim that the new script follows an established wiring pattern in compose-up.sh overstates the precedent.
findings.location: What section — Compose subsection: 'It is WIRED INTO the boot orchestrator infra/scripts/compose-up.sh alongside the existing materialize/.env-regeneration calls, running BEFORE docker compose up (the precedent ordering)'; also AC3: 'The test also asserts infra/scripts/compose-up.sh calls the materialize script before docker compose up'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'the precedent ordering' citation from 'infra/gerrit/materialize-deploy-key.sh / materialize-g2p-config.sh' to 'infra/gerrit/materialize-g2p-config.sh' (the actual compose-up.sh precedent); note that materialize-deploy-key.sh follows a different invocation path (setup-replication.sh).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify and explicitly name the exact `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` Terraform attribute and key format for static header injection (e.g., `request_parameters = { "append:header.X-Opcert-Guard" = random_password.opcert_guard.result }`), and add a structured AC assertion that the key in the plan matches the expected format.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that the `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` resource can inject a static `X-Opcert-Guard` request header, but does not cite or verify the exact Terraform attribute name and key format required. The `request_parameters` attribute on `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` uses a non-obvious key syntax (e.g., `append:header.X-Opcert-Guard`) that is distinct from REST API mapping expressions; an incorrect key (e.g., `header.X-Opcert-Guard` or `overwrite:header.X-Opcert-Guard`) is silently accepted by `terraform apply` while never injecting the header — leaving the inner origin-guard perimeter absent with no deploy-time signal. There is zero prior art for `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` anywhere in `infra/terraform/`.
findings.location: What section — 'maps it as a static request-header parameter (`X-Opcert-Guard`) on the API Gateway integration'; AC1 — 'the box-origin integration with the static `X-Opcert-Guard` request-header mapping'
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section and AC1, cite the exact HCL: `request_parameters = { "append:header.X-Opcert-Guard" = random_password.opcert_guard.result }` (or the operator-seeded SSM value reference). Add to the AC1 jq command a check that the integration's `request_parameters` map contains the `append:header.X-Opcert-Guard` key.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the exact Terraform attribute and mapping-key syntax for injecting the static origin-guard header on `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` (e.g., `request_parameters = { "overwrite:header.X-Opcert-Guard" = random_password.opcert_guard.result }`) so the executor cannot silently misconfigure it.
findings.finding: The plan describes injecting the origin-guard as 'a static request-header parameter (X-Opcert-Guard) on the API Gateway integration' but never names the Terraform attribute (`request_parameters`) or the required AWS mapping-key syntax (`"overwrite:header.X-Opcert-Guard" = random_password.opcert_guard.result`). The `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` resource uses a specific colon-delimited mapping syntax for header overrides; an executor who guesses a plausible-sounding attribute name (e.g., `request_headers`) will produce a `terraform validate`-passing file that silently injects nothing.
findings.location: What section — Terraform bullet, origin-guard header provisioning path; AC1 — 'box-origin integration with the static X-Opcert-Guard request-header mapping'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What/Terraform bullet: 'In the `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` resource, inject the guard header via `request_parameters = { "overwrite:header.X-Opcert-Guard" = random_password.opcert_guard.result }`.' Also add an AC1 test assertion that the plan JSON shows this attribute on the integration resource.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit key-loss recovery runbook (or section in the deploy doc): generate new keypair → `aws ssm put-parameter --overwrite` to re-seed → update `trusted_environments.yaml` with new public key + new `added_at_log_position` — and note that `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` means Terraform will never auto-restore a deleted parameter.
findings.finding: The private Ed25519 key generation and SSM seeding is an explicitly irreversible operation ("the key exists nowhere else"), but the plan provides no documented recovery procedure for accidental SSM parameter deletion or Terraform state loss — the trust anchor would be permanently broken with no back-out path.
findings.location: ## What — SSM Parameter Store / Deploy + prove section; AC bullet 1 (lifecycle { ignore_changes })
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `docs/runbooks/opcert-key-recovery.md` (or a section in `infra/terraform/README.md`) documenting: (1) detect: service returns signing errors / public key mismatch; (2) recover: `ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -N "" -f /tmp/opcert-ed25519`; `aws ssm put-parameter --name /rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key --value "$(cat /tmp/opcert-ed25519)" --type SecureString --overwrite`; shred local copy; (3) re-pin: update `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` with new public key and new `added_at_log_position`. Reference this runbook from the AC operator-attestation comment template.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a key-rotation and key-retirement runbook (even as a brief ordered list in the plan or a linked ADR) covering: (1) generate new keypair, (2) re-seed SSM, (3) update `trusted_environments.yaml` with a new `added_at_log_position`, (4) redeploy the compose service to pick up the new key, (5) verify existing consumers tolerate or are notified of the key change.
findings.finding: The plan defines a guard-rotation lifecycle but provides no lifecycle path for rotating or retiring the Ed25519 private key itself (`/rebar/prod/opcert-ed25519-key`). Because the corresponding public key is pinned in `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` (with `added_at_log_position`), a key rotation requires coordinating at least three mutations (SSM re-seed, `trusted_environments.yaml` update, notifying/re-deploying any consumer that cached the old public key) — none of which are described. This leaves the most security-sensitive shared state with a defined CREATE but no UPDATE/RETIRE path.
findings.location: What section — key generation + SSM seeding; AC bullet on `trusted_environments.yaml`; `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` on the key parameter

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State a concrete default or example for `opcert_admin_principal_arns` (e.g. a `default = []` that produces an empty-trust policy for plan/CI validation, or an explicit `-var` flag in the AC2/AC1 proving commands, or a committed `infra/terraform/terraform.tfvars.example` entry).
findings.finding: The plan specifies that `terraform plan` (AC2) must succeed against the live account, but `opcert_admin_principal_arns` is a new required input variable with no stated default value and no example/tfvars entry provided; a `terraform plan` invocation without this variable will fail with a missing-variable error before any resource logic is evaluated.
findings.location: What section — Terraform bullet ('trust policy limited to the principal ARNs supplied via a new variable opcert_admin_principal_arns'); Acceptance Criteria AC2
findings.suggested_fix: Either add `default = []` to the variable declaration (documented as 'must be overridden at deploy') or amend the AC1/AC2 proving-command lines to include `-var='opcert_admin_principal_arns=["arn:aws:iam::ACCOUNT:role/operator"]'` so the command is unambiguous.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the leaf ticket's `file_impact` field to replace `{path: 'infra/opcert/', reason: 'Terraform for API GW + Fargate + Secrets Manager'}` with the correct paths and technologies: `{path: 'infra/terraform/', reason: 'API Gateway HTTP API + rebar-opcert-admin IAM role + SSM SecureString parameters'}` and `{path: 'infra/compose/', reason: 'opcert compose service + guarded nginx /opcert/ route + materialize-opcert-guard.sh'}` and `{path: '.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml', reason: 'pin deployed env public key + env_id'}`, consistent with both the parent's runtime decision and the leaf's own plan body.
findings.finding: The leaf ticket's `file_impact` structured field lists `infra/opcert/` with reason 'Terraform for API GW + Fargate + Secrets Manager', which references both a path (`infra/opcert/`) and technologies (Fargate, Secrets Manager) that the parent epic explicitly REJECTS in its runtime-decision resolution ('Fargate/Secrets-Manager/KMS-CMK stack rejected') and that the leaf's own plan body explicitly excludes. The correct paths are `infra/terraform/` and `infra/compose/`. This stale metadata predates the runtime decision and diverges from the parent's settled design.
findings.location: Leaf ticket 76d2 `file_impact` field: `{path: 'infra/opcert/', reason: 'Terraform for API GW + Fargate + Secrets Manager'}`
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace the single stale file_impact entry with three entries matching the leaf's actual scope:
1. `{"path": "infra/terraform/", "reason": "API Gateway HTTP API v2 + rebar-opcert-admin IAM role + SSM SecureStrings (opcert-ed25519-key, opcert-origin-guard) + hashicorp/random provider"}`
2. `{"path": "infra/compose/", "reason": "opcert compose service + guarded nginx /opcert/ route + infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh + infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template"}`
3. `{"path": ".rebar/trusted_environments.yaml", "reason": "pin deployed environment public key and env_id with added_at_log_position"}`

findings.finding: The plan's file_impact metadata (ticket field) lists `infra/opcert/` as the Terraform target path, but the plan body consistently targets the existing `infra/terraform/` directory for all new HCL resources. No `infra/opcert/` directory exists or is described anywhere in the plan text — this is a stale/wrong metadata entry that could misdirect an executor scanning the ticket's file_impact field.
findings.location: Ticket file_impact metadata: {path: 'infra/opcert/', reason: 'Terraform for API GW + Fargate + Secrets Manager'}; plan What section (Terraform)
findings.suggested_fix: Update file_impact to: [{path: 'infra/terraform/', reason: 'extend existing module: API Gateway HTTP API, IAM role, SSM parameters, random provider'}, {path: 'infra/compose/docker-compose.yml', reason: 'add opcert service'}, {path: 'infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template', reason: 'add /opcert/ guarded route'}, {path: 'infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh', reason: 'new: materialize guard value from SSM'}, {path: '.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml', reason: 'pin deployed env public key'}]

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Constrain the `random_password` resource for the origin guard to a safe character set for nginx string literals (e.g., `special = false` and `override_special = ""`, or `min_special = 0`) so the generated value can never break the nginx `map` block syntax.
findings.finding: The plan uses `random_password` (Terraform's hashicorp/random provider) to generate the origin-guard value, which is then embedded literally as a string match value in an nginx `map` block directive: `map $http_x_opcert_guard $opcert_guard_ok { default 0; "<value>" 1; }`. Terraform's `random_password` defaults to including special characters (double-quotes, backslashes, curly braces, semicolons). A double-quote or backslash in the generated value will break the nginx config file syntax, causing `nginx -s reload` to fail, which on this shared box takes down the entire proxy (Gerrit web UI, review-bot, and opcert routes). The plan specifies no character-set constraint on the `random_password` resource.
findings.location: What section: 'Terraform generates the guard value with `random_password`' and the nginx map block specification; Compose/nginx wiring paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: Add `special = false` (or equivalently `override_special = ""`) to the `random_password.opcert_guard` resource declaration in the Terraform IaC. A hex or base64url value (e.g., `length = 32, special = false`) is sufficient as an opaque shared secret and is nginx-safe.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Constrain the `random_password` resource generating the origin-guard value with `special = false` (or an explicit `override_special = ""`) to avoid nginx map-directive syntax-breaking characters, and note this constraint in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan uses `random_password` (Terraform hashicorp/random provider) to generate the origin-guard value and inject it as a static nginx `map` directive value. `random_password` includes special characters by default (e.g. `!`, `@`, `#`, `\`, `"`, `;`). Several of these — particularly backslash, double-quote, and semicolon — would break the nginx `map` block syntax (`map $http_x_opcert_guard $opcert_guard_ok { default 0; "<value>" 1; }`) or the nginx config parser, causing `nginx -t` to fail and the service to not start. The plan specifies no `special = false` constraint or escaping mechanism on the `random_password` resource.
findings.location: What section — Terraform bullet, origin-guard header provisioning path; Compose section — materialize script writes nginx map block
findings.suggested_fix: In the Terraform origin-guard provisioning bullet, add: 'The `random_password` resource sets `special = false` to produce an alphanumeric-only value safe for direct interpolation into the nginx `map` directive without escaping.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` exits non-zero on any SSM error and that `compose-up.sh` treats this as a fatal error (aborting before `docker compose up`), and document the manual recovery step (re-run the materialize script once SSM is available).
findings.finding: The materialize-opcert-guard.sh script's error handling on SSM failure is unspecified: if the `aws ssm get-parameter` call fails at boot or during a guard rotation, compose-up.sh's response (abort vs. continue with deny-all default) is not defined.
findings.location: What section — 'On the box the guard value is materialized from its SSM parameter by infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh'; compose-up.sh wiring
findings.suggested_fix: Open the script with `set -euo pipefail`; in compose-up.sh, call the materialize script without error suppression so a failure propagates. Add a one-line note in the runbook: 'If materialize fails, re-run after SSM recovers before retrying compose-up.sh'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify that the API Gateway integration URI MUST use the DNS hostname form (`https://rebar.solutions.navateam.com/opcert/`) — not the bare Elastic IP — because API Gateway validates the backend TLS certificate and Let's Encrypt does not issue IP SANs.
findings.finding: The API Gateway HTTP_PROXY integration URI is described as 'https:// to the box's HOST-nginx TLS origin' at the 'Elastic-IP-bound hostname', but never specifies whether the URI uses the Elastic IP address or the DNS hostname (rebar.solutions.navateam.com). API Gateway validates TLS certificates on backend HTTPS integrations — a bare https://<Elastic-IP>/opcert/ URI will fail TLS validation because Let's Encrypt certificates are not issued for bare IP addresses (no IP SAN). Only the https://rebar.solutions.navateam.com/opcert/ form would pass cert validation.
findings.location: What section — Terraform bullet, integration URI; AC1 — 'box-origin integration with the static X-Opcert-Guard request-header mapping'
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section and AC1, replace 'Elastic-IP-bound hostname' with the explicit URI: `integration_uri = "https://rebar.solutions.navateam.com/opcert/"`, and add an AC3 test assertion that the Terraform integration URI matches this hostname form (the AC3 test already checks `https://` scheme; extend it to assert the full hostname).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Persist the guard-rotation runbook as a committed file (e.g., `infra/runbooks/opcert-guard-rotation.md` or a section in `infra/terraform/README.md`) that is shipped as part of this story's deliverables, documenting the required step order, the brief 403 window, and the verification command.
findings.finding: The guard-rotation runbook (two-step: terraform apply -replace then re-run materialize script) is documented only in the plan prose and is never written to a persistent, operator-discoverable file — operators performing rotation 6+ months post-merge have no canonical reference unless they locate this ticket.
findings.location: ## What — Compose/nginx section, rotation runbook paragraph; ## Scope
findings.suggested_fix: Add `infra/runbooks/opcert-guard-rotation.md` with: Step 1 — `terraform apply -replace=random_password.opcert_guard` (updates SSM + API Gateway header); Step 2 — `ssh rebar-gerrit sudo /infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh` (rewrites nginx map + reload); verify: `curl -H 'X-Opcert-Guard: wrong' https://<origin>/opcert/` → 403. Note the 403 window between steps is expected and fail-closed. Reference from the AC operator-attestation comment.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add Terraform resources for API Gateway HTTP API access logging (a `aws_cloudwatch_log_group` and `access_log_settings` on the stage) and at minimum a CloudWatch metric alarm on 4xx/5xx error rate for the `/opcert/` route, and add an AC asserting these resources exist in the Terraform plan output.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new externally-facing API Gateway endpoint but specifies no observability instrumentation (access logging, CloudWatch metrics, or alerting) for it.
findings.location: What section — API Gateway HTTP API declaration; Acceptance Criteria
findings.suggested_fix: In the Terraform module add: `resource "aws_cloudwatch_log_group" "opcert_apigw_access"` with a 30-day retention, wire it into `aws_apigatewayv2_stage.default.access_log_settings.destination_arn`, and add a `aws_cloudwatch_metric_alarm` on `5XXError` for the API. No new fixed cost (CloudWatch Logs at this volume is effectively free).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a rollback step to the deploy runbook (and to the materialize script or compose-up.sh) that runs `nginx -t` before `nginx -s reload` and aborts on failure, and document the manual rollback: revert `rebar.conf.template` to the pre-story version and reload.
findings.finding: No rollback procedure is specified for the modification to `infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template`, which is a shared config that serves the live Gerrit service; a syntax error or bad render would break all nginx-proxied routes, not just /opcert/.
findings.location: What section — 'infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template — which today has NO /opcert/ location or include — gains both the include … and a location /opcert/ block'; Acceptance Criteria AC3
findings.suggested_fix: In the materialize script and any step that writes the nginx config, gate the reload: `nginx -t && nginx -s reload || { echo 'nginx config test failed — aborting reload'; exit 1; }`. Document in the story's deploy runbook: 'To roll back nginx: `git checkout HEAD~1 -- infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template && sudo cp … /etc/nginx/ && nginx -s reload`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` writes the nginx map to a temp file first and renames it atomically (`mktemp` + `mv`) before calling `nginx -s reload`, so a partial write never leaves nginx with an unparseable config file.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify that `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` writes `/etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf` atomically (write-to-temp + `mv`). If the script is interrupted mid-write or two invocations race (e.g., manual rotation overlapping a reboot), nginx may reload against a partially-written or zero-byte map file, causing a config-parse failure and leaving the guard check in an undefined state.
findings.location: What section — compose/nginx wiring; `infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh` description
findings.suggested_fix: In the script spec, add: `tmpfile=$(mktemp /etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf.XXXXXX) && printf 'map $http_x_opcert_guard $opcert_guard_ok { default 0; "%s" 1; }\n' "$guard_value" > "$tmpfile" && mv "$tmpfile" /etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf && nginx -s reload`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether the `hashicorp/random` / `random_password` approach is necessary, or whether the origin-guard value can be generated once by the operator (e.g. `openssl rand -hex 32`) and stored via `aws ssm put-parameter --overwrite` into a `value = "CHANGEME"` / `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` placeholder — the same established pattern as all 12 other project secrets — thereby avoiding the new provider dependency entirely.
findings.finding: `hashicorp/random` (`random_password`) is introduced as a new Terraform provider with zero existing call-sites in `infra/terraform/` (Rule-of-Three: 0/3), while the codebase's established pattern for generating and storing all 12 other secrets is operator-seeded out-of-band via `aws ssm put-parameter --overwrite` into a `value = "CHANGEME"` placeholder with `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` — a pattern that is uniformly present in ssm.tf, user_data.sh, fetch-secrets.sh, and multiple runbooks, and that would work for the origin-guard value as well.
findings.location: What section — 'the hashicorp/random provider is ADDED to infra/terraform/versions.tf required_providers'; infra/terraform/versions.tf; infra/terraform/ssm.tf
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `random_password.opcert_guard` with an operator-seeded SSM parameter following the established pattern: add `/rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard` to the `rebar_secret_params` local in ssm.tf (or declare it as a separate `aws_ssm_parameter` with `value = "CHANGEME"` + `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }`). The operator generates the guard value once (`openssl rand -hex 32`) and seeds it via `aws ssm put-parameter --overwrite`. The API Gateway integration reads the value from SSM at apply time via a `data "aws_ssm_parameter"` data source. Rotation becomes `aws ssm put-parameter --overwrite` + `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` re-run — identical to the documented rotation procedure for every other project secret, and no new Terraform provider is needed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `infra/compose/Dockerfile.opcert` (and optionally `src/rebar/opcert_service/`) to `BOT_PATHS` in `infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh` so changes to the opcert service trigger an auto-redeploy, or explicitly document that the opcert service is intentionally excluded from autodeploy and explain why.
findings.finding: infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh's BOT_PATHS variable (line 55) is an unenumerated consumer: it lists Dockerfile.reviewbot as the only Dockerfile trigger for auto-redeployment, but the plan adds infra/compose/Dockerfile.opcert to the compose stack. Without adding Dockerfile.opcert to BOT_PATHS, a change to that Dockerfile would not trigger an auto-redeploy of the opcert service on the box.
findings.location: infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh line 55 / What section (Compose wiring)
findings.suggested_fix: In infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh line 55, extend BOT_PATHS: `BOT_PATHS='src/rebar/ infra/compose/Dockerfile.reviewbot infra/compose/Dockerfile.opcert pyproject.toml infra/compose/docker-compose.yml'`. Note that autodeploy.sh currently rebuilds only the BOT_SERVICE (review-bot); if the opcert service needs its own rebuild target, the autodeploy logic may need a second service-rebuild arm or the existing arm must be generalized.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the false claim 'an HTTP API has no resource policy' with the actual design rationale for using identity-only policy (e.g., 'a resource policy would require listing all caller ARNs directly and re-applying on rotation; the identity-policy-on-admin-role approach keeps the caller ARN list in one variable and is sufficient for this single-role use case') — or add a resource policy as defense-in-depth if the design warrants it.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'an HTTP API has no resource policy' as a factual technical constraint justifying why access control is implemented exclusively via an identity policy on the IaC-created `rebar-opcert-admin` role, with no resource-policy layer. This claim is factually incorrect: AWS API Gateway HTTP APIs (v2) do support resource-based policies. The plan conflates 'HTTP API v2 has no separate `aws_apigatewayv2_authorizer` resource for IAM auth' (true — authorization_type=AWS_IAM is a route attribute) with 'HTTP API has no resource policy at all' (false). Because the rationale for omitting a defense-in-depth resource policy rests on a false technical impossibility rather than a deliberate tradeoff, an implementer who checks AWS docs will find the capability exists and be confused about the design intent — or worse, add one inconsistent with the plan.
findings.location: What section: 'Caller restriction is identity-policy-based (an HTTP API has no resource policy)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'an HTTP API has no resource policy' with: 'A resource policy on an HTTP API v2 is available but adds no isolation benefit here beyond the identity policy — the IaC creates the only role with Invoke rights and the caller must assume it — so the simpler identity-only approach is chosen.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the plan's claim that 'an HTTP API has no resource policy' — HTTP API v2 does support resource policies — and replace it with the real justification (e.g., 'identity policy on the IaC-created role is sufficient for this trust model; a resource policy is an available option but is not added here to avoid dual-maintenance').
findings.finding: The plan states 'an HTTP API has no resource policy' as an architectural fact and uses it as the sole justification for a purely identity-policy-based access model. This claim is factually incorrect: AWS API Gateway HTTP APIs (v2) do support resource-based policies (the `aws_apigatewayv2_api` resource exposes a `policy` argument in the Terraform AWS provider v5.x). While the identity-policy approach is still valid and works correctly, the stated justification is wrong — an implementer who investigates and finds HTTP APIs do support resource policies may correctly doubt the entire access-control rationale.
findings.location: What section — 'Caller restriction is identity-policy-based (an HTTP API has no resource policy)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace: 'Caller restriction is identity-policy-based (an HTTP API has no resource policy)' with: 'Caller restriction is identity-policy-based; an `aws_iam_role_policy` granting `execute-api:Invoke` scoped to this API's execution ARN on the `rebar-opcert-admin` role is sufficient — a resource policy is available for HTTP APIs but is not added here as the identity-policy grant already enforces least-privilege access.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add API Gateway access logging (`access_log_settings` with a CloudWatch Logs log group) and at least one CloudWatch alarm (e.g., on `4XXError` or `5XXError` rate) for the new opcert HTTP API, consistent with the existing monitoring.tf pattern for new infra.
findings.finding: No API Gateway access logging, CloudWatch metrics, or health alarms are specified for the new opcert HTTP API endpoint. The existing monitoring.tf covers EC2 host and Gerrit health, but the new internet-reachable service has zero observability in the plan.
findings.location: What section — 'Terraform — extend the existing infra/terraform/' / no monitoring subsection; AC1, AC2 (no logging/alarm AC); infra/terraform/monitoring.tf (existing, host-scoped)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the access-control posture for the Terraform state bucket (`rebar-tfstate-896586841071`) relative to the origin-guard secret: either (a) document that state-read access is restricted to the same principals as `opcert_admin_principal_arns` (i.e., the guard is no weaker than the IAM boundary), or (b) add a note that the EC2 security group should be restricted to API Gateway managed prefixes rather than 0.0.0.0/0 so the origin is not publicly reachable regardless of guard knowledge.
findings.finding: The origin-guard value (`random_password.result`) is a security-critical shared secret that lives in Terraform state as cleartext JSON, protected only by S3-side encryption on `rebar-tfstate-896586841071`. The EC2 host is internet-accessible on port 443 (network.tf: `cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"]` on port 443), and the origin-guard is the SOLE control preventing an adversary from bypassing API Gateway and calling `/opcert/` directly. The plan never declares who may read the Terraform state file or what access controls exist on the state bucket — so the confidentiality boundary for the guard secret is entirely unspecified. Anyone with S3 GetObject on the state bucket can recover the guard value and directly invoke the origin.
findings.location: What section — origin-guard provisioning path; AC1 (random_password / SSM parameter); infra/terraform/versions.tf backend "s3" block
findings.suggested_fix: Add a plan note declaring that the Terraform state bucket's IAM/bucket-policy restricts GetObject to operator principals only — making the guard's confidentiality model explicit. Alternatively, restrict the EC2 SG port 443 to API Gateway's managed prefix list (`com.amazonaws.us-east-1.execute-api`) so the origin is not reachable from arbitrary internet addresses even if the guard leaks; this is the stronger control and eliminates the need for the guard to be kept secret for confidentiality (it becomes a CSRF-like defense-in-depth rather than a primary secret).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add or reference a new ADR (e.g. `docs/adr/0048-opcert-api-gateway-origin-pattern.md`) capturing the API GW HTTP API v2 + AWS_IAM SigV4 + host-nginx-TLS-origin + origin-guard-header pattern and the cost/isolation trade-offs, and link it from `infra/terraform/` (e.g. a comment in the new gateway file).
findings.finding: No new ADR is committed to for the novel API Gateway HTTP API v2 + SigV4 + host-nginx-TLS-origin + origin-guard-header architecture, which is a significant new pattern not previously established in infra/terraform/ or infra/nginx/.
findings.location: Why / What sections; references to ADR 0046, ADR 0047, and epic's 'Producer-signing & compute-runtime design resolution'
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/0048-opcert-api-gateway-origin-pattern.md` documenting: choice of HTTP API v2 over REST API, IAM auth via route attribute (no separate authorizer), private-origin guarding via random header over VPC Link, and why Fargate/NLB/KMS was rejected. Add a comment `# See ADR 0048` at the top of the new Terraform file.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify the choice of `hashicorp/random` over the established operator-seeded SSM pattern (or adopt the established pattern), and if retaining `random_password`, add a pinned version constraint in `required_providers`.
findings.finding: The plan introduces `hashicorp/random` (`random_password`) as a new Terraform provider dependency with zero existing call-sites in `infra/terraform/` (Rule-of-Three: 0/3), while the codebase's established prior art for all 12 SSM secrets is operator `aws ssm put-parameter` + Terraform `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }` with a 'CHANGEME' placeholder. The plan provides no justification for why the origin-guard value should be Terraform-managed (via `random_password.result`) rather than following the established operator-seeded pattern — especially given the plan itself acknowledges this value IS rotation-managed (via `terraform apply -replace=`), which is functionally equivalent to an operator put-parameter.
findings.location: What section — 'the origin-guard header provisioning path: Terraform generates the guard value with `random_password` — the `hashicorp/random` provider is ADDED to `infra/terraform/versions.tf` `required_providers`'
findings.suggested_fix: Follow the established pattern: declare the parameter with `value = "CHANGEME"` + `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }`, and document the operator seeding step as `openssl rand -base64 32 | aws ssm put-parameter --name /rebar/prod/opcert-origin-guard --type SecureString --overwrite --value $(cat -)`. This avoids a new provider, keeps the rotation runbook consistent with the existing pattern, and requires no lock file changes.

findings.finding: The jq proving command in AC1 asserts `.[0].name == "opcert-admin-invoke"`, where `.name` is the Terraform resource label (from `terraform show -json`'s resource-level `.name` field), not the IAM policy name attribute. The What section names only the IAM role (`rebar-opcert-admin`) and describes the inline policy semantics, but never states that the Terraform resource label for the `aws_iam_role_policy` must be `opcert-admin-invoke`. An executor implementing the What section without reading the AC assertion code may choose a different resource label (e.g. `rebar_opcert_admin_inline`) and produce a correctly secured configuration that nonetheless causes the AC1 jq command to exit non-zero.
findings.location: AC1 jq proving command / What section (IAM subsection)
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the What section's IAM bullet: 'The `aws_iam_role_policy` resource is labeled `opcert-admin-invoke` in Terraform (resource label, not only the IAM policy name) so the AC assertion matches.' Alternatively, change the AC1 jq to `.[0].s.Sid == "OpcertAdminInvoke"` or check `.values.name` — whichever is the intended anchor.

findings.finding: The plan claims the origin-guard rotation loop is 'closed' by `terraform apply -replace=` (which updates the SSM SecureString and the API Gateway static header), but the nginx check-value at /etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf is only refreshed when materialize-opcert-guard.sh is explicitly re-run. After a guard rotation, API Gateway sends the new guard header but nginx still validates against the old value, 403-ing all requests until the operator manually re-runs the materialize script and reloads nginx. This is a hidden manual step the plan omits.
findings.location: What section — Compose/nginx wiring; origin-guard provisioning path: '…closing the loop'
findings.suggested_fix: In the rotation description, add: 'after `terraform apply -replace=random_password.opcert_origin_guard`, run `infra/scripts/materialize-opcert-guard.sh` on the box (via SSM Session Manager) to pull the new guard value into /etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf and reload nginx.'

findings.finding: AC4 references 'with `added_at_commit` per the epic's era protocol' to pin the trusted environment entry in `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml`, but the parent epic's authoritative design resolution (comment #7, 'Security finding #5 folded in') explicitly supersedes the original schema field `added_at_commit`/`revoked_at_commit` (explicit git SHAs on main) with `added_at_log_position`/`revoked_at_log_position` (tickets-branch log positions, stamped by a `rebar trusted-env` helper). The leaf's AC4 schema reference is thus pointing at the revoked field vocabulary — an executor who follows the leaf's AC4 literally will write `added_at_commit` into the YAML, which will be incompatible with readers built to the superseded epic schema.
findings.location: AC4 — `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` pinning requirement; parent epic 6d0d-7580-e18f-48c3 comment #7
findings.suggested_fix: In AC4, replace 'with `added_at_commit` per the epic's era protocol' with 'with `added_at_log_position` (the current tickets-branch tip, stamped by `rebar trusted-env`) per the epic's revised era-validity protocol (security finding #5 resolution)'. Also add the corresponding `revoked_at_log_position: null` field to the pinned entry.

findings.finding: The plan references the 'era protocol' as a processing rule that governs how `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` entries are validated at key-validity-check time, but the two conflicting protocol definitions (original 'Trust config & era-validity protocol' in the epic description vs. the later 'Authoritative era anchor' in epic comment #7) are not reconciled or cross-referenced within the leaf plan itself. An executor reading only this leaf cannot determine which protocol is authoritative for the boot-time pinning step (the leaf says 'per the epic's era protocol' with no disambiguation), and the leaf's own test assertion (AC4: 'a test asserts the entry parses and the `env_id` matches') does not verify the era field name at all — making the protocol gap undetectable by the specified test.
findings.location: AC4 (test assertion clause); What section ('pin the environment's public key + `env_id` into `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` per the epic's era protocol')

findings.finding: Leaf AC4 uses `added_at_commit` as the era-boundary field when pinning the deployed environment's public key in `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml`, but the parent's authoritative post-round-6 security-finding-#5 resolution SUPERSEDES the original 'Trust config & era-validity protocol' and renames those fields to `added_at_log_position` / `revoked_at_log_position` (tickets-branch log positions, not explicit git SHAs on main). The leaf's AC4 therefore contradicts the parent's current schema.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC4: '(with `added_at_commit` per the epic's era protocol)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '(with `added_at_commit` per the epic's era protocol)' in AC4 with '(with `added_at_log_position` stamped by `rebar trusted-env` at pin time, per the epic's post-finding-#5 era-at-storage-anchor protocol)'.

findings.finding: The nginx config template gains `include /etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf;` (evaluated at nginx startup time), but `/etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf` is created by `materialize-opcert-guard.sh`, which runs only as part of `compose-up.sh`. Since HOST nginx starts as a system service at boot — before `compose-up.sh` runs — a rebooted or freshly provisioned box will fail to start nginx, breaking all existing services (Gerrit, etc.) because the included file does not yet exist. The What section describes both changes (template gains the include; script runs before docker compose up) without acknowledging this ordering contradiction.
findings.location: What — 'Compose' subsection ('infra/nginx/rebar.conf.template … gains both the include /etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf; directive') and boot-orchestrator description ('BEFORE docker compose up … finishes with nginx -s reload')
findings.suggested_fix: Commit a minimal stub `/etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf` (e.g. `map $http_x_opcert_guard $opcert_guard_ok { default 0; }`) into the repo under `infra/nginx/` and ensure it is deployed to the host as part of IaC/bootstrap (e.g. via a Terraform `null_resource` remote-exec or an Ansible task), so nginx can always start. The materialize script then overwrites the file with the real value and reloads nginx. This eliminates the chicken-and-egg problem and gives a safe default (all requests rejected at the guard check until the real value is seeded).

findings.finding: Adding `include /etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf;` to `rebar.conf.template` creates a boot-ordering hazard: on a box restart, systemd starts host nginx (which loads rebar.conf including the include directive) BEFORE `compose-up.sh` runs `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` (which creates the file). If the file is absent, nginx fails to load (`include` of a non-existent file is a fatal nginx config error), taking down the entire reverse proxy (including Gerrit). The plan specifies `nginx -s reload` at end of the materialize script but does not address the initial-boot file-absent case.
findings.location: What section — Compose/nginx wiring: 'the script writes `/etc/nginx/opcert-guard.conf`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a line to `materialize-opcert-guard.sh` that is also called during initial provisioning (before `systemctl enable --now nginx`), or pre-create the file with a sentinel `map $http_x_opcert_guard $opcert_guard_ok { default 0; }` (no passing value) so nginx loads safely before the real value is seeded.

findings.finding: The plan asserts that Terraform's `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` resource can inject a static `X-Opcert-Guard` value as a request header parameter (mapping `random_password.result` into the integration's `request_parameters`). For HTTP_PROXY integrations (`integration_type = "HTTP_PROXY"`), API Gateway v2's `request_parameters` supports overrides for headers, querystring, and path — but the specific behavior when the value is a static Terraform-interpolated string (not a `$request.` or `$context.` mapping expression) is a non-obvious capability. The plan does not cite AWS documentation or the Terraform registry to confirm this works as described.
findings.location: What section — 'injecting a shared origin-guard header … the integration URI … injecting a static request-header parameter (`X-Opcert-Guard`) on the API Gateway integration'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC1: 'the test asserts the integration's `request_parameters` block includes `append:header.X-Opcert-Guard: "<value>"` (or the AWS mapping-expression equivalent) and cite the Terraform registry `aws_apigatewayv2_integration` `request_parameters` documentation confirming static literal values are supported in HTTP_PROXY integrations.'

findings.finding: The AC1 structured test's jq assertion checks `.[0].name == "opcert-admin-invoke"` (hyphen-delimited), but in `terraform show -json` planned values the `.name` field for a resource is the Terraform local label, which by universal TF convention uses underscores (e.g. `opcert_admin_invoke`). If the implementer follows TF naming conventions the test will exit non-zero — not because of a security violation but because of a label-spelling mismatch — making the self-failing test fail for the wrong reason and undermining AC1's assurance value.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1, jq command: `.[0].name == "opcert-admin-invoke"`
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `{name: .name, …}` with `{name: .values.name, …}` in the jq pipeline (so `.name` refers to the AWS IAM policy's name attribute, which CAN be `opcert-admin-invoke` with hyphens), or add a `Sid` to the policy statement and check `.s.Sid == "OpcertAdminInvoke"` instead.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
